title: Scenario: Raising in the Reciprocal Ranges Game

lines.text: Chapter 7, section 2. Raising in the reciprocal ranges game.
Take the full-street game from chapter three and hand the caller a new option: the raise.
Suddenly, the bettor cannot just bet and expect to be met with a call or a fold.
They have to account for the possibility that their bet might be met with a re-raise.
That new pressure forces the bettor to think about how often they can actually continue after a raise.
Meanwhile, the raiser needs to build a range that makes sense: strong hands and carefully chosen bluffs.
Your strongest hands — the ones that beat almost everything the bettor can hold — usually prefer to raise.
Slow-playing by calling with your best hands lets the bettor off easy and gives you no extra money.
Raising with them charges the bettor to continue and builds a pot when you are likely to win it.
But if you raise only value hands, a thinking opponent folds to every raise and you collect only small pots.
So you add bluffs to the raising range — hands that have little chance of winning at showdown but plenty of fold equity.
The exact ratio of value raises to bluff raises mirrors the same math we saw with betting: it has to make the bettor indifferent to calling.
And because raises now exist, the bettor has to widen what they are willing to bet in the first place — the whole game gets richer.
